articles.title: HANDLING BILLIONS OF INVOCATIONS – BEST PRACTICES FROM AWS LAMBDA
articles.summary: Lambda manages asynchronous requests using internal queues and addresses challenges like noisy neighbors and traffic spikes through techniques like simple queueing, consistent hashing, and shuffle-sharding. Shuffle-sharding, which randomly assigns tenants to multiple queues, is quite effective for workload isolation and fault tolerance. The AWS team has multiple methods in practice for smooth Lambda function operations, including proactive detection, automated isolation, resilience, failure handling, and many observability metrics.

articles.title: MEMORY SAFETY FOR WEB FONTS
articles.summary: Chrome replaced FreeType with Skrifa, a Rust-based font processing library, to improve memory safety and reduce security vulnerabilities related to web fonts. FreeType is written in unsafe code and has a history of security issues that cost Google significant resources to address. Skrifa uses Rust's memory safety features and has rigorous testing to provide a more secure and reliable solution.

articles.title: LIFE ALTERING POSTGRESQL PATTERNS
articles.summary: This article goes over a few important PostgreSQL patterns. For example, it recommends using UUID primary keys as the tradeoffs are usually worth it. Also, `created_at` and `updated_at` timestamps should always be automatically added in tables and `on update restrict on delete restrict` for foreign key constraints should always be set. Enum tables should be used for controlled values, tables should be named singularly, and soft deletes should be used instead of real, hard deletes.

articles.title: WHY AI WILL NEVER REPLACE HUMAN CODE REVIEW
articles.summary: AI will not fully replace human code review because humans understand context, need collaboration, and provide accountability. AI can assist with routine scanning and identifying potential issues, but it lacks the domain expertise, forward-thinking alignment, and ability to learn from experience that human reviewers possess. Also, code review serves as a valuable platform for teaching, learning, and building a shared understanding of the system for teams.

articles.title: WHY I'M NO LONGER TALKING TO ARCHITECTS ABOUT MICROSERVICES
articles.summary: Debates about microservices are often unproductive. The term "microservice" doesn't have a consistent definition, which results in confusion and abstract discussions disconnected from actual business goals. Also, adopting microservices without accompanying organizational changes, such as cross-functional teams and decentralized decision-making, makes the change pointless.

articles.title: HOW I USE AI CODING TOOLS AS A RAILS DEV
articles.summary: This Rails dev uses LLMs for debugging, code completion, and code generation. He prefers RubyMine, but mentions that Cursor has potential and sees a future of seamless AI collaboration within IDEs.

articles.title: CODEMCP
articles.summary: Codemcp is a tool that integrates Claude Pro with a local codebase, allowing AI to directly implement features, fix bugs, and refactor code with file editing and test execution. The main benefit of it is that it uses Claude Desktop instead of the Claude API, which makes it much cheaper comparatively. Codemcp is IDE agnostic and is configured via `codemcp.toml` for defining commands like formatting and testing.

articles.title: NODE MODULES INSPECTOR
articles.summary: An interactive tool that lets you visualize your node_modules and inspect dependencies of your local project.

articles.title: MCP SERVER CLOUDFLARE
articles.summary: This is a Cloudflare MCP (Model Context Protocol) server that allows devs to manage and interact with various Cloudflare services like Workers, KV, R2, and D1 using natural language commands through an MCP client like Claude Desktop. It has features for managing storage, databases, AI models, and more.

articles.title: LLM AGENTS ARE SIMPLY GRAPH — TUTORIAL FOR DUMMIES
articles.summary: Agents are basically graphs with decision nodes, action nodes, and loops, allowing them to "think," take actions, and learn from results. The tutorial uses PocketFlow, a 100-line framework, to show how to build a basic research agent that can answer questions by searching the web. It breaks down the agent into simple "stations" (nodes) and defines the flow between them.

articles.title: AI'S EFFECTS ON PROGRAMMING JOBS
articles.summary: AI isn't replacing programming jobs but creating more of them by creating a new layer of abstraction. This abstraction will enable individuals with structured thinking skills, but less knowledge of coding languages, to become "software developers" who instruct AI. Overall, software production will increase, but mediocre devs might be replaced.

articles.title: SOFTWARE DEVELOPMENT HAS TOO MUCH SOFTWARE IN IT
COMMON MISTAKES IN RESTFUL API DESIGN
articles.summary: Common mistakes in RESTful API design include inside-out thinking, improper URI definition, misuse of HTTP methods, bad error handling, lack of versioning, and overcomplicated responses.

articles.title: HOW TO GET DEEP TRACES IN YOUR NODE.JS BACKEND WITH OTEL AND DENO
articles.summary: The new version of Deno allows immediate access to logs and traces without additional instrumentation by using the `--unstable-otel` flag, which adds OTel to Node.js easily.

articles.title: SELF GAP
articles.summary: Customizing gaps between specific flex or grid items is not flexible enough - current workarounds include adjusting margins or wrapping elements.
